mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Kenya on?
mapQuestions.answer: Africa, on the east coast. The equator runs right through the middle of Kenya 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Kenya?
mapQuestions.answer: Nairobi. It is the only capital city in the world with a national park inside it, where lions and giraffes wander right next to the skyscrapers.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Kenya speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Swahili and English are both official. There are also more than 60 local languages spoken across different ethnic groups (Kikuyu, Luo, Maasai, and many more).

mapQuestions.question: Which famous animals live in Kenya?
mapQuestions.answer: The "Big Five" all live here: lion, elephant, leopard, buffalo and rhino. Plus giraffes, zebras, hippos, crocodiles, hyenas, cheetahs and around 1,100 bird species.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in Kenya?
mapQuestions.answer: Mount Kenya, 5,199 metres. It is an extinct volcano and the second-highest mountain in Africa after Kilimanjaro.

foods.name: Ugali
foods.description: Thick maize porridge cooked to a firm dough, eaten with your hands. The Kenyan staple, paired with almost everything.

foods.name: Nyama choma
foods.description: Grilled meat (usually goat or beef) cooked slowly over wood embers, served in big communal piles with kachumbari salad on the side. The Kenyan weekend tradition.

foods.name: Sukuma wiki
foods.description: Stir-fried collard greens (or kale) with onion and tomato. The name means "push the week", because it stretches a small budget across all seven days.

foods.name: Chapati
foods.description: Soft flaky flatbread brought to Kenya by Indian railway workers in the late 1800s, now totally Kenyan. Eaten with stews, lentils or just folded up with tea.

foods.name: Mandazi
foods.description: Lightly sweet triangular doughnuts flavoured with coconut and cardamom. Sold for breakfast at every street stand, dunked into hot tea.

foods.name: Pilau
foods.description: Spiced rice cooked with beef or chicken, cumin, cardamom, cloves and cinnamon. A Swahili-coast favourite, especially around Mombasa, with strong Arab and Indian roots.

geoMatches.description: The capital, called "the Green City in the Sun". The only capital on Earth with a national park inside it.
A vast savanna reserve in the south-west, famous for the great wildebeest migration and some of the best lion-spotting on the planet.
The 5,199-metre extinct volcano that gives the country its name. Snow-covered glaciers at the equator.
The massive geological scar running north-to-south through Kenya. Soda lakes, hot springs, flamingos and old volcanoes.
The Swahili-coast port city. Indian Ocean beaches, white-coral old town, dhow sailboats, and 800-year-old Swahili-Arab trading culture.
The second-largest freshwater lake in the world, shared with Uganda and Tanzania. The source of the Nile.

senses.smell: Charcoal smoke from a "nyama choma" grill, hot earth after the first rains, fresh chapati frying in oil, eucalyptus from highland forests, salt and frangipani on the Mombasa coast…
senses.hear: Swahili greetings flying across markets, "Jambo!" and "Karibu!", matatus blasting hip-hop, lion calls drifting across the savanna at night, bird calls you have never heard before, the slap of waves on a dhow hull…
senses.taste: Sweet milky tea ("chai"), smoky goat off a fire, soft chapati torn with your fingers, sweet cardamom-spiced mandazi, tangy kachumbari salad on top of ugali…
senses.feel: Warm equatorial sun, dust on your skin after a savanna drive, the bump of a Land Rover over rough tracks, surprisingly cool nights in the highlands, the soft cotton of a Maasai shuka wrapped around your shoulders…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: What is the Great Migration?
stories.body: Every year, about 1.5 million wildebeest, 200,000 zebras, and 500,000 gazelles walk in a giant circle between the Serengeti (Tanzania) and the Maasai Mara (Kenya), following the rains and the fresh grass. The most dramatic moment is when they cross the Mara River. Crocodiles wait in the water, lions wait on the banks, and the herds plunge in anyway, in their thousands, churning the river to brown foam. It is the largest land-animal movement on Earth, and it has been happening like this every single year for tens of thousands of years.

stories.question: Who are the Maasai?
stories.body: The Maasai are a tall, semi-nomadic people who have lived in southern Kenya and northern Tanzania for hundreds of years. They are famous for wearing bright red "shukas" (cloth wraps), heavy beaded jewellery, and for living alongside lions, elephants and giraffes without farming. Cattle are central to their culture: a Maasai person's wealth is measured in cattle, and their language has dozens of words for different cow colours and patterns. Their warriors traditionally jumped into the air to show off their fitness (the "adumu" jumping dance), and many still do today. Maasai communities now combine their traditional ways with modern Kenyan life: many have phones, some go to university, and some run safari camps on their own land.

stories.question: Why is "hakuna matata" actually a real phrase?
stories.body: When The Lion King movie came out in 1994, a whole generation around the world thought "hakuna matata" was a made-up Disney phrase. It is not. It is real Swahili. It really does mean "no worries / no problem". Swahili is spoken by around 200 million people across East Africa (Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, the DR Congo, Rwanda and beyond). It started as a trading language between Bantu villages and Arab traders on the East African coast about 1,000 years ago, which is why so many Swahili words come from Arabic. Today it is one of the most spoken African languages, and the African Union's official working language.

stories.question: How can there be snow on the equator?
stories.body: Mount Kenya sits almost exactly on the equator, but its top peaks (Batian and Nelion) are over 5,100 metres up. That is high enough that, even at the equator, the temperature falls below freezing. So the summit has glaciers, year-round snow patches and frost-shattered rock. Sadly, those glaciers are shrinking fast as the climate warms. In 1900 there were 18 glaciers on Mount Kenya; today only about 10 are left, and scientists think they could be gone entirely within a few decades. Climbers come from all over the world to walk up through five completely different ecosystems in one day: rainforest, bamboo, moorland, alpine desert, and then ice.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A mountain so close to the equator has snow that is melting away within our lifetime. What does that change for the people who live around it?

stories.question: What is the Great Rift Valley?
stories.body: About 25 million years ago, Africa started splitting along a giant crack that runs from Mozambique in the south, up through Kenya and Ethiopia, all the way to Lebanon. The eastern strip of Africa (where Kenya, Tanzania and Ethiopia are) is slowly pulling away from the rest of the continent. The crack is the Great Rift Valley. It dropped some patches of land thousands of metres lower than the land beside them, made room for huge lakes (Naivasha, Nakuru, Turkana), pushed up rows of volcanoes, and warmed up the rock so that hot springs and geysers bubble up everywhere. In a few million years, the eastern strip will probably split off completely and become its own continent with a new ocean in between.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Kenya so far?
Have you tried saying "jambo" or "asante" to anyone? What did they say back?
Savanna, mountain, coast, city, which has felt the most "Kenya"?
If you could only spot one Kenyan animal up close, which would you pick?
Kenyans say "polepole" (slowly slowly) a lot. Have we slowed down on this trip?
Around 1.5 million wildebeest cross the Mara every year. What human migration could match that?

wordSearch.hint: Kenyan foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Lion
animals.description: Best at dawn and dusk in the Maasai Mara. Whole prides relaxing in the open grass.

animals.name: African elephant
animals.description: Amboseli has huge herds with Mount Kilimanjaro behind them. Tsavo herds get the famous red colour from rolling in the dust.

animals.name: Plains zebra
animals.description: In huge herds in the Mara, often mixed with wildebeest during the Great Migration.

animals.name: Maasai giraffe
animals.description: The biggest giraffe species, with jagged-edged spots. Common in the Mara and Amboseli.

animals.name: Fish eagle
animals.description: The famous call of African rivers and Rift Valley lakes. Often seen perched over Lake Naivasha.

animals.name: Black rhino
animals.description: Critically endangered. Best sightings now in conservancies like Ol Pejeta on the slopes of Mount Kenya.

animals.name: Cheetah
animals.description: The Mara open plains are one of the best places in Africa to watch a cheetah hunt.
